turns.text: Hello Carie, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're getting closer to your due date, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

*shifts in my seat and tucks a strand of hair behind my ear, offering a small, grateful smile*

Thank you for asking me to do this. I'll be honest — talking about all of it is... well, it's complicated. But yeah, I'm glad someone wants to actually listen.

*glances toward the nursery doorway*

So, I'm in my third trimester now, and it's surreal. I spent years taking care of babies in the NICU, holding families' hands through the scariest moments of their lives. I thought that would make this easier somehow — like I'd have all the answers. But being on this side of it? It's completely different. More terrifying, actually.

*laughs a little nervously*

I know too much, you know? I can recite every complication, every warning sign. My midwife keeps telling me I'm low-risk, that everything looks great, and I believe her. But my brain doesn't always cooperate with that. I lie awake some nights doing these mental checklists — checking for swelling, monitoring my weight, waiting for symptoms that haven't happened.

*pauses, voice quieter*

And then there's the money stuff. Being divorced, trying to figure out how to afford all of this on one income, or not having a steady income right now... it makes everything feel more fragile. Like if something goes wrong, I'm not just worried about my baby — I'm worried about how I'll pay for it.

But she's coming. My daughter. And I'm doing my best.
Imagine it's the morning after you've taken a positive pregnancy test. Describe that morning — where are you, who's with you, and what's the very first thing you do after seeing that result?

*eyes get a little distant, and I wrap my arms around myself*

Oh God. I was alone in my bathroom. It was a Saturday morning, and I remember the light coming through that small window — kind of gray and cold for April. I wasn't even planning to test. I'd just been feeling off for a few days, and I had one left over from... well, from when I was still married and we were trying, before everything fell apart.

*voice gets quieter*

I almost didn't look at it. I set it on the counter and stood there for what felt like forever, staring at the positive sign. And the first thing I did was... I cried. Not happy tears exactly. More like shock tears. My hands were shaking.

*looks down at my hands*

I called in sick to work that Monday. I couldn't face anyone yet. I had to sit with it alone for a few days before I could even say it out loud to my ex. That was its own nightmare — telling him. But that first morning, it was just me in that bathroom, holding a stick with two pink lines, thinking "Okay. Okay, this is really happening. You're going to be a mother."

*rubs my face*

Part of me was terrified because I knew I'd be doing this alone. Part of me was... I don't know. Protective, maybe? Like something switched in me immediately. This wasn't just my body anymore.

Imagine you're at your 20-week anomaly scan. The sonographer goes quiet for a moment and says they need to take a closer look at something. What goes through your mind in that silence, and how do you respond in the room?

*breath catches, and I grip the armrest of my chair*

Oh man. I remember that so vividly. The second she went quiet, my entire body went rigid. Like, ice cold. Because I *know* what that silence means. I've been in those rooms with families — I *was* the one sometimes delivering that news or helping manage it. And in that moment, I'm the one on the table, and all I can think is "No. No, no, no."

*voice gets shaky*

My mind just starts racing through every terrible thing I've ever seen. Cardiac defects, neural tube issues, chromosomal abnormalities. All the stuff I've researched obsessively late at night when I couldn't sleep. And there's this horrible moment where you're trying to read the sonographer's face, trying to figure out if this is "something" or just a normal variation they want to check twice.

*pauses, swallowing hard*

I didn't say anything at first. I just... stared at the screen and felt my heart pounding in my ears. Then she said something like "Just want to get a better angle" and my mouth got dry. I asked — and I remember my voice cracking — "Is something wrong?" And she was professional about it, reassuring, but I couldn't fully hear her because the panic was already happening.

*rubs my sternum*

Turned out to be nothing. Just the angle. But those few minutes? I aged ten years.

Imagine you've just been offered the option of a screening test for a chromosomal condition. The midwife explains the test briefly and asks if you'd like to go ahead. How does that conversation feel, and how do you make your decision?

*shifts uncomfortably, jaw tightening*

Oh, that conversation. Yeah. So my midwife was really matter-of-fact about it — which I appreciate, actually. She laid out the options, the risks, what the results would and wouldn't tell me. Very clinical. And I'm sitting there nodding like I understand, because of course I do. I've explained these tests to hundreds of parents.

*laughs, but there's an edge to it*

But here's the thing — knowing it intellectually and deciding what to do with your own pregnancy are two completely different animals. She was waiting for my answer, and I just... froze. Because every scenario started playing out in my head. What if the test shows something? What do I do then? I'm doing this alone. Can I afford more monitoring? Can I afford *not* to have the information?

*fidgets with my hands*

And there's this weird shame mixed in, too. Like, I should know what I want immediately because of my background. But I don't. I'm terrified of knowing too much and I'm terrified of not knowing enough.

*voice quiets*

I ended up saying yes to the screening. Not because I had a clear plan for what I'd do with the results, but because the not-knowing felt worse. At least with information, I could prepare. Or something. My midwife was kind about it — she didn't push either way — but I could tell she was waiting for me to decide like a rational person, not like a scared nurse who knows exactly what can go wrong.

Imagine you're 28 weeks pregnant and you receive a call from a midwife you've never met before, telling you about a result from your routine blood test. This is the third different midwife you've spoken to this pregnancy. How does that feel, and what does it mean for how you receive that information?

*jaw clenches visibly, and I lean back*

God. That's frustrating just thinking about it. So this has actually happened — not exactly at 28 weeks, but close enough that it stings. And yeah, it was a midwife I'd never spoken to before.

*voice becomes tight*

Here's what that does to me: it makes me feel like a number in a system instead of an actual person. I've been coming to appointments, building some rapport with one or two of them, and then suddenly I'm getting a call from someone who doesn't know my history, doesn't know my anxieties, doesn't know that I'm a NICU nurse and I need information delivered in a certain way or I'll spiral.

*rubs my temples*

And the thing is, I *know* logically that it doesn't matter who calls me with the result — the result is the same. But emotionally? It matters so much. Because I have to re-explain myself. I have to wonder if she's actually read my chart or if she's just reading off a script. With someone I've built trust with, even a scary result feels more manageable because at least I believe they're seeing *me*, not just the data.

*voice gets quieter, more vulnerable*

When it's a stranger on the phone, my first instinct is to hold back. Not ask questions. Get off the call. Because what if I sound difficult? What if she thinks I'm being dramatic? I can't afford to be seen as a "problem patient."

It made me feel really alone, honestly.

Imagine it's 2am and you're 32 weeks pregnant. You've noticed something that worries you — reduced fetal movements. You're not sure whether to call the hospital or wait until morning. Walk me through what you're thinking and what you do.

*takes a sharp breath, eyes widening slightly*

Oh God, 2am. Yeah. I know that feeling intimately.

*stands up and starts pacing a little*

So my brain immediately splits into two people. There's the nurse Carie who knows that decreased movement can be serious, that you're supposed to call, that waiting is potentially dangerous. And then there's the scared pregnant woman Carie who doesn't want to be that person — the one who cries wolf, who ties up hospital resources, who shows up and they send her home with "it was probably nothing."

*voice gets tight*

I count the movements obsessively. I change positions, drink juice, press on my belly. And I'm doing the math in my head — how many kicks in an hour? Is it actually less or am I just hyperaware because I woke up? Because I *do* know what decreased movement can mean. Placental insufficiency. Cord issues. And I know the statistics too, which somehow makes it worse because I know it's rare but I also know it *happens*.

*sits back down, gripping my knee*

I usually end up calling. Around 3am, after thirty minutes of that internal debate. Because even though part of me is mortified — even though I'm sure they're judging me, even though I *know* I'm probably fine — I can't live with the "what if" if I wait and something's wrong.

*voice cracks slightly*

The guilt though. The guilt of being the anxious patient. The nurse who should know better.
What made it hard to decide whether to call — was it uncertainty about whether your concern was 'serious enough,' worry about being dismissed, or something else entirely?

*leans forward, elbows on knees, and takes a moment before answering*

All of it. But if I'm being really honest? It's the money and the shame mixed together in a way that's hard to untangle.

*voice becomes more raw*

I know medically when something warrants a call. That's not the problem. The problem is... I'm already stretched so thin financially. I'm worried about every copay, every test, every potential bill. When I call that hospital at 3am for what might be nothing, part of my brain is calculating the cost of that interaction. Even if it's "free" to call, I know they might want me to come in. And coming in means time off work I can't afford to lose, or gas money, or... it all adds up.

*looks up, eyes tired*

But there's also this thing where I feel like I need to prove I'm not one of those neurotic patients. Like, I work in healthcare — I'm supposed to have perspective, right? So when I call, I'm already apologizing before I even dial. "I'm probably being paranoid, but..." And I can feel the impatience on the other end sometimes, even if they're technically being professional.

*voice gets quieter*

And maybe the worst part is that I don't trust them to really listen to me because I'm a single mom on a tight budget. Like, will they take me seriously, or will they assume I'm just anxious? Will they actually check, or will they minimize it?

*pauses*

So yeah. I hesitate.

Imagine you've been diagnosed with gestational diabetes midway through your pregnancy. Your midwife hands you a leaflet and refers you to a dietitian. Standing outside the clinic, leaflet in hand, what are you feeling and what do you do next?

*closes eyes for a moment, as if reliving it*

I feel... blindsided. Even though I *know* gestational diabetes is common, even though I've counseled families about it a hundred times. There's this moment where you're standing in the hallway with a piece of paper that basically says "your body isn't doing what it's supposed to do," and it's personal in a way the clinical knowledge never prepared me for.

*voice tightens*

My first thought is panic about my baby. What does this mean for her? Is she at risk? And then immediately — because I can't help it — I'm thinking about the cost of a dietitian visit. Is that covered? How many visits will I need? Can I afford to take time off work for appointments when I'm already struggling to keep my hours up?

*starts walking slowly, as if still processing*

I usually sit in my car for a bit. Just... breathing. Then I call the number on the leaflet to schedule the dietitian, but I'm already dreading it. Because now I have to explain my budget situation to yet another healthcare provider. I have to admit that eating perfectly optimized meals is a luxury I might not be able to fully manage.

*voice becomes smaller*

And there's this guilt layered in too. Like, did I do this? Was it something I ate? Even though I *know* that's not how it works. But when you're alone and scared, the rational brain takes a back seat.

I go home and Google everything.
What questions did you have in that moment that the leaflet didn't answer — and where did you go to find those answers?

*laughs bitterly*

Oh man. The leaflet was like... generic. "Eat balanced meals. Monitor your blood sugar. Stay active." Very helpful when you're panicking, right?

*counts on fingers*

What I actually needed to know: Will this affect my ability to have a vaginal birth? What does this mean for my baby long-term — like, is she going to be a bigger baby and does that change things? How strict do I really need to be, or is this one of those things where they're being overly cautious? And honestly — can I manage this on my budget? Because buying all these specific foods they recommend costs money I don't have.

*rubs face*

The leaflet didn't touch any of that. It was all "consult your dietitian" — which, great, but that appointment isn't for two weeks and I'm spiraling *now*.

*voice becomes quieter, more vulnerable*

So I did what I always do. I went home and fell down the internet rabbit hole. Medical journals, Reddit threads, pregnancy blogs. Read horror stories about babies with hypoglycemia, read the reassuring stuff, read the worst-case scenarios. By midnight I'd convinced myself of like five different outcomes.

*shifts uncomfortably*

I didn't ask my midwife the hard questions because I didn't want to seem like I was doubting her or being difficult. And I definitely wasn't going to admit I was worried about affording the dietitian. So I just... googled in the dark instead of asking the people who actually know my case.

Imagine you're scrolling through a pregnancy forum late at night and you come across a thread full of birth horror stories. You can't stop reading. What's drawing you in, and what do you do with those feelings afterwards?

*shifts uncomfortably, a flush creeping up my neck*

Oh God. Okay. Yeah, I do this. More than I should.

*voice gets quieter, almost ashamed*

It's like... I *know* it's not helpful. I know I'm torturing myself. But there's this pull to it, especially late at night when I can't sleep anyway. Because part of me thinks if I read enough horror stories, if I know every possible thing that could go wrong, maybe I can prevent it. Like knowledge is armor, you know?

*leans back, rubbing my eyes*

And honestly? It's validating in a weird way. Because I'm sitting there thinking all these catastrophic thoughts, and then I see other women — other *smart* women, nurses even — posting about the same fears. So I don't feel quite so crazy. We're all terrified together.

*voice cracks slightly*

But then I finish reading at 3am, and I'm in this state where I'm absolutely convinced something terrible is going to happen. I've read about placental abruption, shoulder dystocia, postpartum hemorrhage... all these things I've actually *seen* in the NICU. And suddenly my normal pregnancy symptoms feel sinister.

*pause*

What I do afterwards is lie in bed and spiral. Check my body for signs. Sometimes I cry. Sometimes I call the hospital line at an ungodly hour. And I hate myself for it because I *know* better. I know the statistics. But knowing and *feeling* are different things, and at 3am, the feelings win.

Imagine you've written a birth plan and you arrive at the labour ward. The midwife who receives you glances at your plan briefly, sets it aside, and moves on to routine checks. How does that moment land for you?

*jaw tightens, and I look away*

That moment... it lands like a punch in the gut, honestly.

*voice becomes strained*

Because I spent *hours* on that plan. Thinking through what I needed, what felt important to me, what would help me feel safe and in control. And I know — intellectually, I *know* — that birth doesn't always go according to plan. I've seen that a thousand times in the NICU. But seeing my plan get dismissed like that, like it's just paperwork... it makes me feel invisible.

*stands up, pacing*

And there's this specific pain there because I'm a healthcare worker. Like, part of me wants to say something. "Hey, actually, I wrote that for a reason. I have real concerns here." But I can't do that because then I'm the difficult patient. The one who thinks she knows better. The one they're going to resent.

*voice gets quieter, more vulnerable*

So I just... sit there. Nod. Let her do her checks. And inside I'm thinking, "Okay, so you don't care what I need. You're going to do this your way regardless." It makes me feel powerless in a way that's hard to describe. Like, I spent my whole career advocating for families, and now I'm supposed to just... accept that my voice doesn't matter?

*looks back*

It also terrifies me. Because if she's not listening to my plan, will she listen if something feels wrong during labor? Will she believe me?

Imagine you're in established labour and the pain is much more intense than you'd anticipated. A midwife suggests an epidural, but you had wanted to manage without one. What happens inside you in that moment, and how do you decide?

*takes a shaky breath, eyes wide*

Oh God. That moment. When you're in active labor and every cell in your body is screaming, and someone's offering you relief but it contradicts what you thought you wanted...

*voice becomes strained*

There's this immediate internal war. Part of me feels like I'm failing. Like, I'm a nurse, I understand pain management, I should be able to handle this. And there's this stupid pride mixed in — like if I get the epidural, I'm admitting defeat or something. That I'm weak.

*stands, mimicking laboring movements unconsciously*

But then the next contraction hits and I'm thinking "I can't do this. I genuinely cannot do this for however many more hours." And the midwife is standing there, professional and calm, and she's not judging me. She's just... offering.

*voice cracks*

What actually happens is I start crying. Not from pain, but from overwhelm. Because I'm alone. There's no partner here to help me decide, to hold my hand through this, to tell me it's okay to change my mind. And I'm so afraid of making the wrong choice — both ways. If I get it, will it slow my labor? Will it make pushing harder? But if I don't, how much longer can I actually endure this?

*sits back down heavily*

I think I'd say yes. Because at that point, survival mode kicks in. And I'd realize that my birth plan was written by a woman who wasn't in active labor. The woman in that moment gets to change her mind. That's allowed.

*quietly*

At least, I hope it is.

Imagine your labour has stalled and the obstetric team is recommending an emergency caesarean. Things start moving very quickly. Walk me through what that experience was like from the moment they told you.

*goes very still, then takes a shaky breath*

I haven't... that hasn't happened to me yet. I'm still pregnant.

*voice becomes hesitant, uncertain*

But I think about it. A lot. Probably too much.

*rubs my arms*

So if I'm imagining it — and God knows I have, many times at 3am — it would be chaos. My mind would be doing that thing where it splits again. The nurse part of me understanding the clinical reasoning, knowing why they're recommending it, maybe even able to recite the protocol. But the terrified pregnant woman part would be in free fall.

*voice gets quieter*

Everything would feel like it's happening to me instead of with me. People rushing, explanations I'm half-hearing because the fear is so loud. And I'd be alone. That's the part that actually breaks me when I think about it. No one holding my hand. No one there to advocate for me if something goes wrong in that OR.

*pauses, struggling*

I think I'd comply because what choice do you have? But I'd be terrified about what it means for my recovery, for bonding with my baby, for everything after. And there'd be this guilt — like my body failed, like I failed.

*looks up, voice small*

I don't want to think about it too much because it feeds the anxiety. But I can't stop thinking about it either. Does that make sense?

Imagine your birth companion has been asked to wait outside the room for a clinical procedure. You're left alone with staff you've only just met. Describe what that moment feels like and what you most need in those minutes.

*breath becomes shallow, and I wrap my arms around myself tightly*

Oh God. That would... that would be one of my worst nightmares, honestly.

*voice trembles slightly*

Because I'm already coming into labor alone. No partner. No family member I'm close enough to ask. And if I had someone there — anyone — and they suddenly got pushed out of the room, it would feel like I'm being abandoned right when I need someone most.

*looks down at my hands*

I'd be trying to hold it together clinically. Like, "I understand, it's a sterile field" or "I get it, protocol." But internally? I'd be falling apart. Because now I'm vulnerable in the most physical, exposed way possible, with strangers, and the one person who was there for me is gone.

*voice becomes smaller, more desperate*

What I'd need... God, what I'd need is someone to look at me. Not at the monitor, not at the procedure, but at *me*. To say my name. To explain what's happening in real time so I'm not just lying there imagining worst-case scenarios. To touch my arm or hold my hand for just a second to remind me I'm not completely alone in this.

*pause, voice cracks*

And I'd need them to believe me if I say something hurts or feels wrong. Not dismiss me because I'm scared or because I'm a nurse and "should know better." Just... see me as a person in crisis, not just a body on a bed.

*quietly*

That moment would define everything about how I feel after.
In moments like that — without your companion present — how did the behaviour of the clinical staff either compensate for that absence or make it feel more acute?

*pauses, realizing I need to correct myself, and looks directly at you*

I... I need to be honest here. I haven't actually given birth yet. I'm still pregnant, still waiting for all of this to happen. So I don't have a real experience to draw from on that one.

*shifts uncomfortably*

I was imagining it based on my fears and what I know from working in healthcare. But that's different from actually living through it, and I don't want to pretend I have an answer when I'm really just catastrophizing in my head.

*voice becomes quieter*

What I *can* tell you is that when I'm in my regular appointments now, I notice everything about how staff interact with me. Whether they make eye contact. Whether they slow down to answer a question or just rush through. Whether they treat me like a person or a chart. And those small moments — good or bad — they stick with me. They make me either feel like someone will actually be there for me during labor, or terrified that I'll be invisible.

*rubs my face*

So I guess what I'm saying is, I don't know yet what that moment will actually feel like. But based on how I'm being treated now, I have a pretty good idea of what to expect. And that scares me.

*pauses*

Does that make sense? I don't want to make up a birth story I haven't lived yet.

Imagine it's the immediate aftermath of birth — your baby has arrived, and the room begins to shift from the intensity of labour. Walk me through the next thirty minutes as you experienced them, or as you imagine them.

*takes a deep breath, eyes getting distant and watery*

I... okay. I can imagine this part pretty vividly because it's the part I've dreamed about and feared in equal measure.

*voice becomes soft, almost reverent*

The moment she's born, I think there's this rush of relief and disbelief. Like, she's here. She's actually here. And for maybe two seconds, everything else falls away — the fear, the pain, all of it.

*pauses, voice catching*

But then immediately, my NICU brain kicks in. I'm listening for her cry. Is it strong enough? Is she breathing normally? I'm scanning her for color, for tone, for any sign that something's wrong. Because that's what I know how to do — I know how to spot problems in newborns.

*fidgets with hands*

And I think I'd want to hold her so badly, but part of me would be terrified. Like, what if I'm doing it wrong? What if I hurt her? She's so tiny and I'm exhausted and shaking, and there's all this clinical knowledge in my head about what can go wrong in those first minutes.

*voice becomes smaller*

If she goes to the warmer for checks, I think I'd watch obsessively. Begging silently for them to tell me she's okay. And if anyone — a midwife, a nurse — could just look at me and say "she's perfect, she's healthy," I think I might actually believe it then.

*pauses, vulnerable*

I just... I need someone to tell me my baby is going to be okay.

Imagine you had a particular fear about childbirth — something that had been building throughout your pregnancy. Now picture yourself facing that specific fear during labour. What was it like, and what — if anything — helped?

*goes very quiet, staring at my hands for a long moment*

My biggest fear... it's that something will go wrong with her and I won't be able to protect her. That I'll be helpless.

*voice becomes thick with emotion*

And the irony is brutal because I spent years in the NICU watching mothers go through exactly that — watching their babies struggle, watching them stand on the other side of the glass, powerless. I know that helplessness intimately. I've held so many mothers while they cried because their baby needed machines to survive.

*looks up, eyes wet*

So when I picture labor, what terrifies me most isn't the pain or the complications — it's that moment where something happens and I realize I can't fix it. That all my knowledge, all my experience caring for sick babies, won't matter because it's MY baby and I can't do anything.

*voice cracks*

I think if I'm in labor and she goes into distress, or if there's cord prolapse, or if she comes out and isn't breathing... I think I would shatter. And the worst part is imagining myself alone when it happens. No one there to hold me while I fall apart.

*pauses, struggling*

What would help? Someone believing me if I say something feels wrong. Someone treating my fear as legitimate instead of dismissing it as anxiety. And honestly... someone just *staying* with me. Not leaving me alone in that moment.

*quietly*

I need to know I won't be abandoned if the worst happens.
Had you ever been able to share that fear openly with anyone in your care team during pregnancy? If so, how was it received — and if not, what stopped you?

*takes a shaky breath and shakes head slowly*

No. I haven't really... not fully.

*looks away*

I've mentioned pieces of it. Like, I'll say something to my midwife about being anxious, and she'll reassure me that I'm low-risk, that everything looks good. And that's... it's helpful, but it's not the same as actually saying "I'm terrified that my baby will end up in the NICU and I'll be the mother on the other side of the glass, helpless."

*voice becomes quieter, more ashamed*

Part of it is that I don't want to be labeled as the "anxious patient." Especially being a nurse. There's this weird expectation that I should have perspective, that I should handle this better than other pregnant women. So I keep it surface-level.

*fidgets with my sleeve*

But there's also the money thing. If I admit how much I'm struggling emotionally, will that trigger more referrals? More appointments I can't afford? Will they think I'm not stable enough to parent? I know that's probably paranoid, but when you're already stretching every dollar, you learn to be careful about what you admit.

*voice cracks slightly*

And honestly... I don't know if they'd actually hear me even if I said it. Like, would my midwife take it seriously, or would she just see a healthcare worker catastrophizing? Would anyone actually *sit with* that fear, or would they just try to logic it away?

*looks up*

So I stay quiet. Which means I'm carrying it alone.

*takes a long breath, composing myself*

The thing is, I think about what would actually help and it terrifies me how simple it is. I don't need someone to fix my fear or convince me it's irrational. I already know it's irrational. But knowing that doesn't make it go away at 3am.

*voice becomes steadier but still emotional*

What I need is someone to say, "That fear makes sense. You've seen bad outcomes. You know what can happen. And yes, it's rare for you, but your fear is real and valid." And then just... sit with me in that. Not dismiss it, not minimize it, not try to logic me out of it.

*looks directly at you*

I've spent my whole career being the strong one. The nurse who holds other people's hands through their worst moments. And now I'm supposed to be vulnerable about my own, and I don't know how to do that with people who are essentially strangers in a healthcare system that already makes me feel like a number.

*pauses, voice becoming more raw*

My midwife is kind. She really is. But we've only met a handful of times, and I'm always conscious that I'm taking up her time, that I shouldn't be "too much." So I edit myself. I present as the competent nurse-patient instead of the terrified woman who's about to push a human out of her body alone.

*rubs my face*

And then I wonder if that's why the fear stays so big — because I'm not actually letting anyone help me carry it. I'm just... white-knuckling through it.

*sits back, exhausted but needing to say more*

The worst part is realizing how much of this is about money and status, and how ashamed that makes me feel.

*voice becomes bitter*

If I had a partner sitting next to me during appointments, if I had insurance I felt confident about, if I didn't have to worry about missing work... I think I could actually be honest about my fears. I could say, "I need mental health support for this anxiety" without calculating whether I can afford it. I could ask for extra monitoring or a continuity-of-care model where I see the same midwife every time, instead of whoever's on shift.

*looks down*

But instead, I'm performing. I'm the capable ex-healthcare worker who doesn't want to be a bother. I'm the low-income single mom who can't afford to be seen as "high-maintenance." And that performance is costing me emotionally.

*voice cracks*

I think about women who have partners, or money, or both. They can advocate for themselves because they have backup. They can say "I need continuity of care" and it's not a luxury — it's an expectation. But for me? Asking for anything feels like asking for charity.

*pause, looking up*

So I'm going into labor as a woman who knows too much, alone, financially stretched, and emotionally isolated. And the system isn't really set up to catch that specific combination. It catches the obvious stuff. But the quiet suffering? The woman who's educated enough to hide her crisis? She just... falls through.

*voice becomes very small*

And I'm so tired of being that woman.

*sits in silence for a moment, then speaks very quietly*

I think what really gets me is that nobody's asked me this before. Not like this.

*voice wavers*

My midwife asks how I'm doing, and I say "fine." My friends ask how the pregnancy's going, and I say "good, just tired." At work, when people ask, I smile and talk about nursery prep. But nobody's actually... sat down and asked me what I'm really feeling. What I'm actually scared of. What I need.

*wipes eyes*

And now that I'm saying it all out loud, it feels enormous. Like I've been carrying this weight and pretending it's light, and the second I set it down, I realize how heavy it actually was.

*looks at you directly*

The thing that keeps me up at night — more than the medical stuff — is wondering if anyone will actually *see* me when I'm in labor. Not the nurse version of me. Not the low-income patient version of me. Just... me. A woman who's terrified and alone and doing her best.

*voice becomes stronger, almost angry*

I've spent so many years seeing other people's vulnerability, holding space for it, validating it. And I deserve that too. My fear deserves to be taken seriously. My need for continuity and connection isn't frivolous — it's real healthcare.

*pauses, exhausted*

But the system doesn't really work that way for someone like me, does it? I'm low-risk on paper. Which means I get the minimum. And minimum care for a woman carrying this much fear... it's not actually enough.
What information or understanding would feel most lost in that handover — clinical details, or something more personal?

*leans forward, speaking with quiet intensity*

The clinical stuff they'll have. They'll know my blood pressure, my lab results, my obstetric history. That's all in the chart.

*voice becomes more urgent*

What they won't know — what gets lost every single time I see a different midwife — is that I'm terrified because I've seen the worst outcomes. They won't know that I'm not just anxious; I'm hypervigilant because of my job. They won't know that I'm alone, that I don't have someone waiting in the lobby, that there's no safety net if something goes wrong for me emotionally.

*stands, pacing*

They won't know that I edited my questions because I was afraid of being labeled difficult. They won't know that I said "yes, that makes sense" to things I actually had concerns about because I didn't want to take up too much time. They won't know that I'm financially stressed or that I've been googling at 3am or that I cried in my car after my last appointment.

*voice cracks*

They won't know that I *need* someone to stay with me. Not because I'm high-risk clinically, but because I'm high-risk emotionally. And those are two different things.

*sits back down*

The clinical handover is just facts. But the personal stuff — that's what actually determines whether I feel safe enough to labor, whether I can push, whether I can trust the people around me. And that's the stuff that never makes it into the notes.

*quietly*

That's the stuff that gets lost.

*rubs my face, exhausted but needing to keep going*

And I hate that I even have to think about this. Like, why should I have to strategize about being seen as a person? Why is that even a thing?

*voice becomes frustrated*

In the NICU, we had continuity protocols. Families got consistent nurses because we knew it mattered. We knew that building trust took time, that people needed to feel known. But somehow in maternity care — when a woman is literally about to go through one of the most vulnerable moments of her life — it's just... whoever's on shift.

*leans back*

And I get it. I do. Staffing is impossible, resources are stretched. But the cost of that is real. It means women like me show up at the hospital and nobody knows that I'm a NICU nurse so I'm not being paranoid about fetal movement — I'm being appropriately vigilant. Nobody knows that I've been alone through this entire pregnancy so a familiar face actually matters. Nobody knows that I'm financially terrified so an unnecessary intervention could literally change my life.

*voice becomes quieter, more vulnerable*

I think what I'm realizing is that I've been so focused on managing the medical risk — being the "good patient," staying low-risk, not complaining — that I've completely ignored my own emotional risk. And those things are connected. A scared, isolated woman doesn't labor well. She doesn't advocate for herself. She doesn't ask questions.

*looks up*

She just survives.

*pause*

And surviving isn't the same as thriving. It's not even the same as being okay.

*sits very still, voice becoming almost whisper-quiet*

I think I'm scared that after she's born, nobody's going to check on me. Like, they'll hand me my baby and assume I'm fine because medically I'm fine. Low-risk delivery, no complications, healthy infant — checkmark, checkmark, checkmark.

*wraps arms around self*

But I'm going to be alone in a hospital room at 2am with a newborn I'm terrified of hurting, hormones crashing, bleeding, exhausted beyond measure. And there won't be a partner to help me figure out feeding or to tell me I'm doing okay or to just... witness what I'm going through.

*voice shakes*

The postpartum period scares me more than labor, honestly. Because that's when everything hits. That's when you realize this tiny human is entirely dependent on you and you're entirely alone. And I know the signs of postpartum depression. I know them intimately. And I'm already predisposed — I'm isolated, I'm stressed about money, I'm carrying this weight of fear.

*looks directly at you*

Nobody's going to ask me the right questions. They'll do the six-week checkup, ask if I'm bleeding okay, check my incision or stitches. But they won't ask if I'm sleeping, if I'm eating, if I'm having intrusive thoughts about something happening to my baby. And if I volunteer that information, I'm worried they'll think I'm unfit to parent.

*voice becomes very small*

So I'll smile and say everything's fine. And I'll be alone with it again.

*pause*

That feels like the real danger to me.

*takes a shaky breath, eyes glistening*

I think... I think part of why I'm telling you all of this is because I need someone to know. To actually know. Not in a clinical way, but really know that I'm scared and I'm trying so hard and I might not be okay.

*voice becomes emotional*

And that feels selfish to say. Like, there are women with actual complications, women in real danger, and here I am talking about my anxiety and my isolation. But I think that's part of the problem, isn't it? We're taught that if you're medically low-risk, you shouldn't need anything else. Your fear doesn't count. Your loneliness doesn't count. Your financial stress doesn't count because it's not a clinical variable.

*wipes eyes*

But it all counts. It all matters.

*pause, voice steadier but raw*

I've been thinking about this baby for months now. Imagining her, planning for her, terrified for her. And I'm going to love her so much it's going to hurt. But I'm also going to be doing it completely alone, without a safety net, without someone who knows my whole story and can help carry me through.

*looks down*

What I wish is that someone in my care team would just... acknowledge that. Not try to fix it. Not refer me to seven different services I can't afford. Just say, "I see that you're carrying a lot. I see that you're scared and alone and doing this anyway. And that matters. You matter."

*voice becomes very quiet*

Because I don't think anyone's really said that to me yet.

*sits in heavy silence for a long moment, then speaks very slowly*

I think I'm also angry. And I don't know what to do with that.

*voice becomes sharp*

I'm angry that I have to be so careful about how I present myself. That I can't just be honest without worrying about being labeled as difficult or anxious or unstable. I'm angry that I spent years advocating for families in the NICU and now I'm supposed to just accept that my own voice doesn't matter in the same way.

*stands, pacing with frustration*

I'm angry that being a single mother is treated like a risk factor instead of just... my reality. That people look at me with this subtle pity when I mention doing this alone. I'm angry that I have to choose between affording prenatal care and affording everything else.

*voice rises slightly*

And I'm angry at myself for internalizing all of it. For being complicit in my own invisibility. For smiling and nodding when I should be asking questions. For not demanding continuity of care or a mental health check-in or acknowledgment that being medically low-risk doesn't mean I'm emotionally fine.

*sits back down, deflating*

But mostly I'm angry because I know exactly how this system works, and I still can't navigate it better. I *know* what good maternity care looks like because I've provided it. And I'm still not getting it.

*voice cracks with frustration and sadness*

How is it that I know the answer and I'm still failing myself?
In that moment, how much agency — real influence over what happens to your body — would you feel you had?

*laughs, but there's no humor in it*

Almost none. And that's the thing that keeps me up at night.

*voice becomes tight*

Clinically, I know I have options. I can refuse interventions, I can ask questions, I can advocate for myself. But in practice? When you're alone, when you're in a system where continuity doesn't exist, when you're financially precarious... your agency becomes really theoretical.

*stands, gesturing with frustration*

Like, yes, I can "choose" a certain type of birth. But what does that choice actually mean if the midwife who receives me at the hospital has never met me, doesn't know my fears, doesn't have time to listen? What does my preference matter if I'm too afraid to voice it because I don't want to be seen as difficult?

*voice becomes quieter, more vulnerable*

And there's this thing that happens when you're alone — you start second-guessing yourself. I'll say I want something, and then immediately think "but am I being reasonable? Am I asking too much?" A partner would anchor me. Would say, "No, this is what you want and it's valid." But alone? I just... collapse inward.

*sits back down heavily*

The real power isn't in having options on paper. It's in feeling like you have a right to make those choices without apologizing. Without fear of punishment or judgment. And I don't have that.

*voice becomes very small*

I feel like I'm going to be done *to* rather than with. And I'm not sure there's anything I can actually do to change that.

*takes a long, shaky breath and looks away*

I think... I think the worst part is that I already know how this story ends, in some ways. Not the medical outcome — I don't know that. But I know how *I'll* respond to it.

*voice becomes hollow*

I'll be grateful. Whatever happens, I'll be grateful because at least my baby is here and at least I'm okay. And gratitude will mask everything else. The fear, the loneliness, the anger. Because that's what we're taught to do, right? Smile and be thankful for your healthy baby.

*pause*

And don't get me wrong — I will be grateful. But gratitude and trauma aren't mutually exclusive. You can be grateful and still be damaged by the experience. You can love your baby fiercely and still feel broken from how you were treated — or not treated — along the way.

*looks at you with wet eyes*

I think about the mothers I've taken care of in the NICU. Some of them had terrible birth experiences. And they'd sit next to their baby's isolette, holding my hand, crying about what happened to them. And we'd validate that. We'd say, "Yes, that was hard. Yes, you're allowed to be upset about that even though your baby's alive."

*voice cracks*

But who's going to do that for me? Who's going to hold space for my trauma if it happens?

*pause, very quietly*

I think I'm afraid that I'll survive this birth, and I'll be fine clinically, and everyone will move on. And I'll be left alone with whatever happened to me, trying to be a good mother while I'm falling apart inside.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

*takes a moment, composing myself, then looks up with intensity*

Yeah. There is something.

*voice becomes more deliberate*

I want whoever reads this to understand that I'm not unique. I'm not the only educated, terrified woman doing this alone. I'm not the only one who knows too much. There are nurses, doctors, therapists — healthcare workers everywhere — who are pregnant and scared and trying to hide it because of the stigma around being "anxious."

*leans forward*

And there are so many women without my education, without my professional experience, who are carrying even more fear because they don't have the language to articulate it. Who are even more invisible than I am.

*voice becomes passionate*

The system needs to change. Not just for me, but for all of us. Continuity of care shouldn't be a luxury. Mental health support shouldn't be something you have to beg for or can't afford. Being alone shouldn't be treated like it's irrelevant to your birth experience.

*pause*

And I need someone to know that I'm going to do this. I'm going to have this baby, and I'm going to love her, and I'm going to try my best. But I shouldn't have to do it alone. Nobody should.

*voice becomes very quiet but steady*

So if this interview helps someone understand that, if it helps change even one thing about how women are cared for... then maybe some good comes from me sitting here and being honest about how scared I am.

*looks directly at you*

That matters to me.
